title: Coalescence of Polymer Droplets Moving on a Surface with Stiffness Gradient
authors: Divyansh Tripathi, Vimal Kishore, Panagiotis E. Theodorakis, Swarn Lata Singh
abstract: Here, we study the coalescence of two droplets that are moving in the same direction on a soft surface; the motion of the droplets is caused by a gradient in the surface stiffness. As reference, stationary coalescence of the same droplets is also studied on the corresponding uniform surfaces for different stiffness values. To describe the coalescence phenomenon on a surface with stiffness gradient, a relevant range of velocity ratios of the leading and the trailing droplet was considered to elucidate the effect of this parameter on coalescence. Moreover, to analyze the dynamics of the process, the temporal growth of the bridge height $(h)$ was investigated, which follows a power law $(h \sim t^α)$, before eventually attaining a constant value. The obtained values of $α$ show a transition from a higher to a lower value as a function of time, pointing to the presence of two distinct power-law growth regimes, where the transition signifies the crossover from the capillarity-dominated regime to the viscoelasticty-dominated regime of coalescence. In addition, varying attractive strengths for droplet--droplet and intra-droplet interactions were considered. The results indicate that both the dynamics and the degree of the coalescence strongly depend on these interaction parameters. Thus, we anticipate that our results will shed more light on the durotaxis-driven coalescence of polymeric droplets for various relevant system parameters, which will have practical implications for applications ranging from microfluidics to ink-jet printing, where substrate properties may vary. In addition, results may add to the fundamental understanding of the interactions among multicellular aggregates moving on biological surfaces.
summary: This paper studies how polymer droplets merge when moving across a surface with varying stiffness. It reveals that the merging process transitions between different physical regimes (capillarity vs. viscoelasticity) and is highly sensitive to the chemical attractions between the droplets and the surface.
main_problem: Investigating the coalescence dynamics of two viscoelastic polymer droplets moving on a soft substrate with a stiffness gradient (durotaxis).
main_result: The study identifies a crossover from a capillarity-dominated to a viscoelasticity-dominated regime in bridge growth and shows that coalescence morphology and mixing depend strongly on inter- and intra-droplet attraction strengths.
method: Coarse-grained molecular dynamics simulations using the LAMMPS package with a Langevin thermostat and Velocity-Verlet integration.
model_or_system: Two viscoelastic polymer droplets (modeable via FENE potential and LJ interactions) moving on a deformable substrate with a stiffness gradient implemented via a harmonic potential.
key_observables: Temporal growth of bridge height (h) and radius (r), power-law exponents (alpha and beta), contact angle, and degree of droplet mixing.
important_parameters: Substrate stiffness gradient, velocity ratios of droplets, droplet-surface attraction strength, and droplet-droplet/intra-droplet interaction strengths.
main_assumptions: The system is modeled using coarse-grained particles; the effect of droplet bending rigidity is not considered.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the experimental setup of durotaxis motion; Figure 8 shows the distance between droplet centers of mass; Figure 9 shows time-sequenced snapshots of the 'enveloping' morphology.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of durotactic coalescence, describes the molecular dynamics model and simulation setup, compares stationary vs. moving droplets, analyzes the effect of various interaction parameters, and discusses biological implications.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
